replication_study.hypothesis: Participants assigned to the lottery-frame condition will report a lower mean willingness-to-pay (WTP, measured in dollars) than participants assigned to the gift-certificate (gift) frame, after restricting to participants who correctly interpret the instructions (comprehension check == 3).

data_plan.source_type: Online experimental dataset / survey data (cleaned Qualtrics export)
data_plan.wave_or_subset: Single combined dataset for the experimental replication (no waves). Analysis will use subsets defined by comprehension check and WTP thresholds as in the original script.
data_plan.sample_size: Full file contains ~281 observations (rows indexed 1..281 in Data_Cleaned_22102020.csv). The final analysis sample will be the number after applying: (1) non-missing WTP per condition, and (2) comprehension check == 3. Exact analytic sample sizes (N_total, N_check==3, N_check==3 & WTP<=20) will be computed by the provided script prior to hypothesis testing.
data_plan.unit_of_analysis: Individual participant (one row per participant in the CSV).
data_plan.access_details: Dataset is provided within the replication package (data/original/32/input/replication_data/Data_Cleaned_22102020.csv). No further access restrictions. For reproduction in containerized setting, place the file at /app/data/Data_Cleaned_22102020.csv (the Python script expects /app/data/...).

qualification.explanation: This dataset is appropriate because it contains explicit WTP measures for both the lottery and gift frames and the comprehension checks used to select participants who correctly interpreted the instructions. The provided script (Script.R) implements the same critical test used in the original study (comprehension-filtered comparison of WTP between frames), indicating this data is intended to replicate the original experimental comparison.
qualification.similarity_to_original: The original study's focal dependent variable is WTP and the independent variable is frame label (e.g., lottery ticket vs gift certificate). The post_registration.json documents the original study design and outcome: WTP and WTA measured in dollars, between-subjects random assignment to frames (see post_registration.json 'method' and 'outcome_variable'). The replication dataset contains Lot_WTPc and Gift_WTPc variables and Lot_check/Gift_check comprehension items which directly map to the original variables (see replication_data/Script.R lines in the codebook describing these variables and data/original/32/input/post_registration.json for the original's WTP measure and frame manipulation).
qualification.deviation_from_original: The original paper ran multiple separate experiments on MTurk with reported sample sizes (e.g., Experiment 1: N=119; Experiment 2: N=418; etc.) as recorded in post_registration.json. The replication dataset here appears to be a cleaned, combined dataset (Data_Cleaned_22102020.csv) with different sample size (≈281) and may represent a different collection instance or a simulated/cleaned reproduction rather than the exact original raw MTurk data. This makes it a different but comparable sample (different size and possibly different recruitment/time/location) while retaining high fidelity in terms of measured variables and experimental condition mapping (see post_registration.json for original sample sizes and Script.R for dataset variables).

data_plan.notes: Data contains a number of nonstandard entries (dollar signs, very large outliers such as 7000, 200000) in WTP fields; the original R script and the translated Python script strip dollar signs and coerce values to numeric and also include a robustness check restricting WTP <= 20 (as in the R script). Some Qualtrics metadata columns were removed (per Script.R header comment). The AttCheck/AttCheck_10_TEXT fields have mixed-case entries and some missing values; the Python script handles common formatting issues (dollar signs, commas) but further cleaning choices should be documented. The Python translation (Script__py.py) writes results to /app/data/replication_results.txt and saves filtered datasets to /app/data/dataS.csv and /app/data/dataSC.csv.

steps: Run the provided Python script replication_data/Script__py.py (entrypoint) which implements the original R logic: read data, create Cond variable (lottery vs gift), compute WTP from Lot_WTPc/Gift_WTPc, drop missing WTP, set check variable as Lot_check or Gift_check depending on Cond.
Filter to participants with check == 3 (correct comprehension). Save this filtered set as dataS.csv and run the main comparison.
Compute descriptive statistics (N, mean, SD) of WTP by condition for dataS.
Run Welch two-sample t-test comparing WTP in lottery vs gift for dataS (as in Script.R). Compute Cohen's d (pooled SD) as effect-size estimate.
Run a robustness check restricted to those with WTP <= 20 (dataSC subset), compute descriptive stats, and run Welch t-test and Cohen's d for this subset.
Save outputs (replication_results.txt, dataS.csv, dataSC.csv) to /app/data/ and inspect results.
If distributions are strongly non-normal or variances highly unequal beyond what Welch t-test addresses, run nonparametric Mann-Whitney U test as sensitivity check and report both results.
planned_method.models: Two-sample t-test (Welch's t-test) for between-group mean difference; compute Cohen's d effect size (pooled SD). Sensitivity analyses: nonparametric Mann-Whitney U test and/or trimmed means if necessary.
planned_method.outcome_variable: Willingness-to-pay (WTP) in dollars (variable created as WTP from Lot_WTPc or Gift_WTPc).
planned_method.independent_variables: Frame condition (binary: 'lottery' vs 'gift') as constructed from Lot_check and Gift_check indicators in the dataset (Cond).
planned_method.control_variables: Primary replication will not include covariates (matching original planned pairwise comparison). Optionally include demographic covariates (Age, Gen (gender), Inc) as robustness checks if imbalance or covariate adjustment is desired.
planned_method.tools_software: Python 3 (script written for execution). Key Python packages: pandas, numpy, scipy. The original R script is provided but translated for reproducibility in Python (Script__py.py).

planned_estimation_and_test.estimation: Estimated mean difference in WTP between gift and lottery conditions (difference in means) and standardized effect size (Cohen's d).
planned_estimation_and_test.test: Welch two-sample t-test for difference in means (two-tailed by default). Report t-statistic, degrees of freedom estimate, p-value, group Ns, group means and SDs. Sensitivity: Mann-Whitney U if nonparametric needed.

planned_method.missing_data_handling: Listwise deletion for the analytic test: drop observations with missing WTP, and then filter on comprehension check (keep only check == 3). Missing or non-numeric WTP entries are coerced to NA and removed prior to analysis. The script also cleans dollar signs and commas prior to numeric conversion.
planned_method.multiple_testing_policy: Primary test is pre-specified (comprehension-filtered WTP comparison). The <=20 subset analysis is a pre-planned robustness check mirroring the original script; if treated as an additional confirmatory test, report both but indicate the primary test (no formal multiple-testing correction will be applied, consistent with original practice of planned contrasts; if multiple confirmatory comparisons are elevated, apply Bonferroni or Benjamini-Hochberg as a sensitivity).
planned_method.inference_criteria: Two-tailed significance threshold alpha = 0.05 for primary test. Directional hypothesis (lottery < gift) will also be reported with direction of estimated mean difference. Report exact p-values and 95% confidence intervals for mean difference if requested. Emphasize effect sizes (Cohen's d) and confidence intervals rather than strict dichotomous decisions alone.

files.replication_data/Script__py.py: Python translation of the provided R analysis script. Reads /app/data/Data_Cleaned_22102020.csv, performs variable cleaning (removes '$', commas, coerces numeric), constructs Cond (lottery/gift) from Lot_check/Gift_check, computes WTP from Lot_WTPc/Gift_WTPc, filters comprehension check == 3 (dataS), runs Welch t-tests and computes Cohen's d, repeats test for WTP <= 20 subset (dataSC). Writes outputs to /app/data/replication_results.txt, /app/data/dataS.csv, and /app/data/dataSC.csv. This is the script to execute as the analysis entrypoint.
files.replication_data/Data_Cleaned_22102020.c: Cleaned experimental data file with variables used for replication (Lot_WTP, Lot_WTPc, Gift_WTP, Gift_WTPc, Lot_check, Gift_check, demographics, attention checks). The Python script expects the CSV to be available at /app/data/Data_Cleaned_22102020.csv when run inside the execution environment.

codebase.notes: The Python script is intended as the execution entrypoint. It was written to use /app/data for all I/O to comply with the execution environment. The Script.R is preserved but not executed. The Python script includes defensive cleaning of dollar signs and missing data; it reproduces the same filters and tests as the R script. Dependencies are standard scientific Python packages; runtime is expected to be short on CPU-only machines.

analysis.instructions: 1) Ensure Data_Cleaned_22102020.csv is placed at /app/data/Data_Cleaned_22102020.csv. 2) Run Python 3 interpreter and execute replication_data/Script__py.py (the script assumes data at /app/data/...). Example: python replication_data/Script__py.py (working directory can be project root as long as /app/data is mounted). 3) Inspect /app/data/replication_results.txt for group Ns, means, SDs, t-statistics, p-values, and Cohen's d. 4) Inspect /app/data/dataS.csv (comprehension-filtered sample) and /app/data/dataSC.csv (comprehension-filtered & WTP<=20 subset) if further diagnostics are needed. 5) If nonparametric checks are desired, modify Script__py.py to run scipy.stats.mannwhitneyu and save results.
analysis.comparison_metrics: Primary comparison metrics: mean WTP by condition, difference in means, t-statistic and p-value (Welch t-test), Cohen's d (pooled SD). Secondary/sensitivity: Mann-Whitney U test p-value, median difference. Report sample Ns for each condition, and confidence intervals for mean differences where possible.
